title: Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets
author: JK Rowling
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets" book by JK Rowling?
Options:
A. Countless times, Harry had been on the point of unlocking Hedwig’s cage by magic and sending her to Ron and Hermione with a letter, but it wasn’t worth the risk. Underage wizards weren’t allowed to use magic outside of school. Harry hadn’t told the Dursleys this; he knew it was only their terror that he might turn them all into dung beetles that stopped them from locking  him  in the cupboard under the stairs with his wand and broomstick. For the first couple of weeks back, Harry had enjoyed muttering nonsense words under his breath and watching Dudley tearing out of the room as fast as his fat legs would carry him. But the long silence from Ron and Hermione had made Harry feel so cut off from the magical world that even taunting Dudley had lost its appeal — and now Ron and Hermione had forgotten his birthday. 
B. Many times, Harry considered using magic to send his owl Hedwig to Ron and Hermione with a letter, but it was too risky since underage wizards were not permitted to use magic outside of school. Harry hadn't told the Dursleys this; he knew their fear of him turning them into insects was the only thing stopping them from locking him in the cupboard under the stairs. For the first couple weeks back, Harry had enjoyed muttering nonsense to scare Dudley. But with no word from Ron and Hermione, Harry felt disconnected from the magical world, and even scaring Dudley lost its fun - and now they had forgotten his birthday.
C. Again and again, Harry thought about using magic to send his owl Hedwig to his friends Ron and Hermione with a letter. But he knew it was against the rules for underage wizards to use magic outside of school, so he resisted the temptation. The Dursleys didn't know about this rule; Harry was sure their fear of him turning them into dung beetles was the only reason they didn't lock him and his magic things in the cupboard under the stairs. At first when he got back, Harry had liked whispering nonsense words to make Dudley run away scared. But with no letters from Ron and Hermione, Harry felt cut off from the magical world, so even frightening Dudley lost its appeal - and now they had forgotten his birthday.
D. Many times Harry considered using magic to send his owl Hedwig to his friends Ron and Hermione with a letter. But underage wizards were prohibited from using magic outside of school, so it was too risky. The Dursleys didn't know about this rule; their fear that Harry might transfigure them into insects was the only thing stopping them from locking Harry and his magical possessions in the cupboard under the stairs. For the first couple weeks back, Harry enjoyed scaring Dudley by muttering gibberish. But with no word from Ron and Hermione, Harry felt disconnected from the magical world, and even frightening Dudley lost its fun - and now they had forgotten his birthday.
Answer:

Underage wizards weren’t allowed to use magic outside of school. Harry hadn’t told the Dursleys this; he knew it was only their terror that he might turn them all into dung beetles that stopped them from locking  him  in the cupboard under the stairs with his wand and broomstick. For the first couple of weeks back, Harry had enjoyed muttering nonsense words under his breath and watching Dudley tearing out of the room as fast as his fat legs would carry him. But the long silence from Ron and Hermione had made Harry feel so cut off from the magical world that even taunting Dudley had lost its appeal — and now Ron and Hermione had forgotten his birthday.
options.B: Many times, Harry considered using magic to send his owl Hedwig to Ron and Hermione with a letter, but it was too risky since underage wizards were not permitted to use magic outside of school. Harry hadn't told the Dursleys this; he knew their fear of him turning them into insects was the only thing stopping them from locking him in the cupboard under the stairs. For the first couple weeks back, Harry had enjoyed muttering nonsense to scare Dudley. But with no word from Ron and Hermione, Harry felt disconnected from the magical world, and even scaring Dudley lost its fun - and now they had forgotten his birthday.
options.C: Again and again, Harry thought about using magic to send his owl Hedwig to his friends Ron and Hermione with a letter. But he knew it was against the rules for underage wizards to use magic outside of school, so he resisted the temptation. The Dursleys didn't know about this rule; Harry was sure their fear of him turning them into dung beetles was the only reason they didn't lock him and his magic things in the cupboard under the stairs. At first when he got back, Harry had liked whispering nonsense words to make Dudley run away scared. But with no letters from Ron and Hermione, Harry felt cut off from the magical world, so even frightening Dudley lost its appeal - and now they had forgotten his birthday.
options.D: Many times Harry considered using magic to send his owl Hedwig to his friends Ron and Hermione with a letter. But underage wizards were prohibited from using magic outside of school, so it was too risky. The Dursleys didn't know about this rule; their fear that Harry might transfigure them into insects was the only thing stopping them from locking Harry and his magical possessions in the cupboard under the stairs. For the first couple weeks back, Harry enjoyed scaring Dudley by muttering gibberish. But with no word from Ron and Hermione, Harry felt disconnected from the magical world, and even frightening Dudley lost its fun - and now they had forgotten his birthday.
